findings.description: Relying exclusively on color to convey information or distinguish elements, which creates accessibility barriers for users.
findings.definition: "Color alone" refers to the practice of using color as the sole visual means to communicate information, indicate a state, or differentiate between elements within a user interface. This approach is an accessibility violation because it excludes users who are colorblind, have low vision, or are using displays that don't render color effectively. To meet accessibility standards, especially WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines), designers must always provide additional non-color visual cues, such as text labels, icons, patterns, underlines, or distinct shapes, to ensure that all users can perceive and understand the conveyed information.

findings.description: A visual indicator that highlights which interactive element on a page or component currently has keyboard focus.
findings.definition: Focus states provide a crucial visual cue, typically an outline, border, or background change, to show users which element will respond to keyboard input (e.g., Tab, Enter, Spacebar). They are essential for accessibility, enabling users who navigate with keyboards, assistive technologies, or alternative input devices to understand their current position and interact with content effectively. Without clear focus states, keyboard-only users cannot perceive where they are on a page, making navigation and interaction impossible. Best practices dictate that all interactive elements, such as buttons, links, form fields, and navigation items, must have discernible focus states, as mandated by WCAG guidelines, to ensure a usable and inclusive experience.

findings.description: A distinct visual indicator that highlights the interactive element currently receiving keyboard input.
findings.definition: Visible focus is the explicit visual styling applied to an interactive user interface component (such as a link, button, or form field) when it receives keyboard focus. This indicator, often presented as an outline, highlight, or underline, is fundamental for accessibility. It enables keyboard users, including those with motor impairments or those who prefer not to use a mouse, to clearly identify their current position on the page, navigate effectively, and interact with all elements. Ensuring a strong visible focus helps meet accessibility standards and improves overall usability for everyone.

findings.description: Software or hardware tools designed to help people with disabilities interact with digital products.
findings.definition: Assistive technologies (AT) encompass a wide range of software and hardware devices that enable individuals with disabilities to perceive, operate, understand, and interact with digital interfaces. These tools bridge the gap between users with diverse needs and the digital content, providing alternative ways to access information or input commands. Common examples include screen readers for visually impaired users, screen magnifiers, speech recognition software, alternative input devices (such as switch devices or head trackers), and braille displays. Designing user experiences with compatibility for assistive technologies is fundamental to achieving digital accessibility and inclusivity.

findings.description: Short, contextual text that provides guidance, clarification, or examples for a user interface element, typically an input field.
findings.definition: Helper text is a UI component used to assist users in understanding how to interact with an element, such as a form input, by offering additional context, explaining expected input formats, detailing constraints (e.g., character limits), or providing examples. It aims to prevent user errors, reduce cognitive load, and improve the overall usability of a form or interactive component, often appearing persistently below a field or dynamically on focus. This reduces the likelihood of users encountering validation errors or needing to guess the correct input.

findings.description: The process of organizing and controlling the dynamic data within a user interface or application to ensure consistency and responsiveness.
findings.definition: State management refers to the systematic process of organizing, storing, and accessing the dynamic data that defines an application's current condition, often referred to as its 'state.' This includes transient UI states (e.g., whether a modal is open, which tab is active), user input, data fetched from servers, and application-wide settings. For UX, effective state management is fundamental because it ensures that the user interface remains consistent, predictable, and responsive as users interact with it. Poor state management can lead to confusing UIs, unexpected behavior, and a frustrating user experience, while well-managed state supports fluid interactions and a cohesive application feel across various patterns like forms, navigation, and data displays.

findings.description: Visual indicators that highlight the interactive element currently receiving keyboard focus, enabling keyboard navigation.
findings.definition: Focus styles are distinctive visual cues, such as outlines, highlights, or changes in background color, that appear around interactive elements (like buttons, links, and form fields) when they are navigated to using a keyboard (e.g., Tab key). They are crucial for accessibility, allowing users who rely on keyboards, screen readers, or other assistive technologies to clearly see which element is active and ready for interaction, ensuring a predictable and usable experience. Well-designed focus styles are visible and consistent, helping users understand their current position within the interface.

findings.description: The logical sequence in which interactive elements on a user interface receive keyboard focus.
findings.definition: Focus order, often referred to as tab order, dictates the path a user traverses through an interface when using keyboard navigation, such as the Tab key, arrow keys, or screen reader commands. A well-designed focus order aligns with the visual flow of content and interactive components, ensuring that users who rely on keyboards, screen readers, or other assistive technologies can easily understand, interact with, and operate the interface without confusion. Maintaining a logical and predictable focus order is a fundamental aspect of web accessibility and a key usability best practice.

findings.description: The different appearances an interface element or system can take to communicate its current condition, status, or user interactivity.
findings.definition: Visual states are crucial for creating intuitive and responsive user interfaces. They represent how a component or an entire screen looks at any given moment, often changing in response to user actions, system processes, or data conditions. Common visual states include hover, focus, active, disabled, selected, error, success, loading, and empty. By clearly signaling changes in state, visual states provide immediate feedback to users, clarify interactions, and help them understand what's happening within the interface, ultimately improving usability and user satisfaction.

findings.description: Semantic elements are HTML tags that clearly describe the meaning or purpose of the content they contain, rather than just how the content looks.
findings.definition: By using semantic HTML elements such as `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<article>`, `<section>`, `<aside>`, and `<footer>`, developers provide inherent meaning and structure to web content. This approach greatly benefits accessibility by allowing screen readers and other assistive technologies to accurately interpret and navigate the page structure. It also improves search engine optimization (SEO) and makes the codebase more maintainable and understandable for other developers, ultimately contributing to a more robust and inclusive user experience.

pattern guide table vs list vs card
